fif_work_explain: I have colleagues who are both diagnosed and undiagnosed with autism. I also work with students, some of whom are autistic.
tfa_comments_test: I would feel grateful for an opportunity to receive more information (even confirmation that nothing is wrong). That feeling would be immediately followed by the uncertainty of "what if", but I would always rather have more information than not to support my family.
tfa_ethic_details: Ethically, I believe offering access to all babies and families is right. It would be unethical if access was restricted. Morally, families would still have the choice to opt-in or opt-out of the screening, so they can decide what is best for them. For families who want more information about their baby, screening even with no troubling behavior is morally right. It would be morally wrong to force all babies to be screened, especially using some of the more invasive methods shown.
tfa_comments_gain_loss: While I may be stressed, worried, and confused, and my relationship with my baby may be different, those feelings would have still happened later down the road when the more traditional diagnostics take place. It is better for me to know now and be able to adapt and prepare for the future. The good outweighs the bad when it gives me more information to connect to resources, community, and my family sooner.
tfa_comments_regrets: Depending on what type of test was performed (blood test, brain imaging vs. doctor observation), I would have questions on how confident to be in a "change" of results. I think I would regret getting tested if it was a doctor's observation and now my child looks to be a neurotypical kindergartener. I would call that a bad (invalid) test that probably wasted a lot of time and energy for our family. I would feel less confident of a "change" if it was a blood/brain test. I would have more trust in the original results and likely question the new observations. I'd likely wonder if my child is adjusting well and appears neurotypical now, but has not yet been exposed to triggers that would cause them to exhibit signs of autism (stress, puberty, sports/groups, etc). In some ways, I would then be waiting for the signs to appear and that may cause me to regret the early screening test. Either way, a "wrong" test is a risk with anything and there is no way to know at the time of opting-in to the screening. This would be part of my informed consent to participate in the testing and a choice that I (with my family) would make.
tfa_comments_gained_lost: The reverse side of the coin of "Reassurance" is "Relief". While I would feel less anxious and more confident in supporting my baby's future, I cannot say I wouldn't also feel relieved. It's hard not to! This could unintentionally, though, lead to a negative outlook on autism and disabilities ("thank goodness it's not us") and therefore estrangement from the autism community.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: This would again depend on the type of screening test that was done and the level of severity of signs that my child is exhibiting. Either way, though, thinking my child is not autistic during their early years only to find out later that they are autistic is no different from how the current system works.
tfa_explain_freescreen: I believe in science. I believe in making data-informed decisions and utilizing the resources that are available. I also recognize that not everyone believes the same thing, so I would want parents to have the choice.
tfa_whylater: Children at this age exhibit a wider range of behaviors (walking, talking, etc) that may give more indicators to make the test more reliable.

tfa_feedback_other: When it comes to conversations about autism, it's so important to always lead with sympathy, love, and understanding. For us parents, our kids truly are our world.  And when autism comes into the picture, it can sometimes feel very heavy.    Many of us carry guilt and blame ourselves, even though science is clear: autism is not caused by something parents did or didn't do. Still, those feelings are real. That's why the way professionals, friends, and even family talk about it matters - it should be done in a way that uplifts, not weighs down.    Because at the end of the day, what every parent needs most is support, compassion, and the reminder that they're not alone.

tfa_comments_test: I suppose more context would matter. I would feel less surprised and have a kinder range of emotions about this including scars and also happy if I had an older child who already had a diagnosis. If the screen was clearly being offered to all infants and I did not have an affected older child I would have very little emotion or reaction.
tfa_ethic_details: There are many nuances here... if the screen is effective in helping identify children with higher risk for an ASD diagnosis and this leads to better f/u and earlier family support and therapy interventions then that would likely out weigh the risks of the negative emotions a parents may experience through this process. Offering the free screenings with appropriate consent is very ethical. There are also many children who may not have any obvious signs of ASD at 4 mos and develop these characteristics at older ages so lack of delays does not matter. If the child already has delays it is likely that they need more evaluation already than a standard infant screening.
tfa_comments_gain_loss: Knowing as much as possible about your child and what they need to grow and succeed is the primary goal of any parent. A formal diagnosis while hard can provide resources, knowledge, and support for success that would otherwise go unexplored or underutilized. It also helps lessen the impacts of constant comparison to others, may reduce parental guilt, and allow for engagement in a new community where a child or family can be better understood. Stressors will certainly come with an ASD diagnosis but they are not less when it happens later after months or years of struggle and lack of understanding.
tfa_comments_regrets: If we had the diagnosis, had lots of therapies and support and my child no longer met criteria for diagnosis at school age then that is the best possible outcome. I would not regret that.   If  I got the diagnosis, struggled to engage with therapies because of push back from partner, lack of family support, lack of available resources etc and then found out that maybe the test had been wrong all along I would regret having the test in the first place. I would hesitate to implement or consent to a screening for an asymptomatic child if there were not adequate resources and access available to investigate further and provide helpful interventions.  As this relates to the earlier questions this would not serve any helpful purpose and would not be ethical.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: If I ignored symptoms because they were screened so it "can't be Autism" I may regret that in the long term. tfa_explain_freescreen: I like knowledge and understand the role and value of screening tests. I do not think that it should be required until it becomes clear that screening before symptoms leads to a significant change in longterm outcome.

tfa_comments_test: I think the possibility of my child having autism would make me a little nervous. I was not particularly anxious or depressed postpartum, but I could imagine how this could be a little bit of a trigger for some moms. More than that, I would have reservations about the accuracy of autism screening at that very early age. While I think it would be possible to flag children who would have more severe cases, I would be concerned about the possibility of "ruling out" children who may still be autistic. It seems like, when accurate, it would set children up on the best path to get EI services early, but on the flip side it could lead to a delay for kids whose parents thought autism was ruled out. I'd be more comfortable if my pediatrician talked to me about the studies supporting the screening and the limitations on the front end.
tfa_ethic_details: I have slight reservations about this, as the accuracy and the detail the test provides would heavily influence how ethical and moral it is. Some people with autism require little to no intervention, and others would require extensive additional care. Performing a simple "yes or no" test for the presence of autism would not provide much value to the individual child. And of course, as the accuracy declines, the test becomes less worthwhile. On a macro level, I think there could be an inherent value for obtaining the data, but I don't necessarily believe a child should be subjected to tests that could permanently "mark" them as autistic without a medical indication supporting this. At 4 months, it would be hard for me to see why it would be a valuable use of resources. Certainly by 1 year, when children have had more time to develop and parents have a better view of their child's development in terms of milestones, there would be more evidence to support these screens.
tfa_comments_gain_loss: The most important thing gained is context that would help me support my baby's development as he grows into a child and young adult. This would obviously give me the ability to seek out early intervention services, connect with community, find the best schools and resources available, and have more compassion when my child acts in a way that doesn't necessarily make sense for the situation.     My fear would be that this diagnosis could cause me or others to write off my child's perceptions or experiences as "symptoms," rather than giving them the weight they're entitled to.
tfa_comments_regrets: I don't think I'd regret it, but I may regret (1) telling others that my child is autistic when he wasn't or (2) spending my child's early days worrying about this diagnosis and the impacts for him, when the stress wouldn't actually be necessary.
tfa_comments_gained_lost: The only thing that I feel like a parent loses in this scenario is the time and energy spent in the screening. If the child is fussy or irritable, there is a cost to putting the child through the screening. There is also always some risk in blood draws and saliva samples that the child's genetic privacy is being compromised. That said, there are benefits, primarily peace of mind, that come from this screener.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: I think I would feel guilty for missing the signs. I think there would be a voice in my head basically telling me to let it go, and that there's nothing off, when I questioned my child's development or behavior. I would feel awful if believing these wrong results caused me to delay intervention.
tfa_explain_freescreen: I have a family history of autism and I have a general understanding of the importance of early intervention. To me, while there are risks associated with this test, it would make sense to have all of the information possible as early as possible.

tfa_ethic_details: Early prevention helps us plan for the future.
tfa_comments_regrets: The screening still helped us to be proactive by getting our child connected to resources in the community that could help us.
tfa_explain_freescreen: Early prevention screenings help to prepare the community on how best to support those who are differently abled.

fif_diag_firstsigns_what: At about 1 year old we first noticed a delay in expressive speech. subsequently after she was evaluated by Babynet, she was found to have delays in additional areas along with behaviors that we thought might be associated with autism.
fif_describe_child_other: She was originally diagnosed with level 2 autism. Due to her well above average cognitive profile along with parent provided interventions she often appears to be "high functioning" despite the fact that her functional benefactors are very limiting.
tfa_comments_test: It would be irresponsible to evaluate for autism at this age because language and behavior are simply not developed to a degree where there could be reliable and valid diagnostic certainty. I believe that an autism diagnosis at such a young age might lead to a decrease in appropriate outcomes due to cognitive bias.
tfa_ethic_details: Parents should be provided with the option to proceed with this test with informed consent, but I personally don't believe that it would be prudent to test at an age when language and cognitive development wouldn't be anywhere near the level of development that would be necessary in order to observe diagnostic criteria. I believe that false positives in such a test would lead to negative outcomes for some children due to subconscious cognitive biases.
tfa_comments_gain_loss: I have gained the ability to plan for and actuate early interventions. Please see above for the losses that I would anticipate.
tfa_comments_regrets: Although I think this scenario is unlikely based simply on the lack of symptomology being present over such a long period of time, I can't imagine that the early interventions applied would outweigh the potential harm
tfa_comments_gained_lost: I feel as if I would view autism as an unwanted outcome that had been avoided and I don't believe that we should further stigmatize our children.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: I was provided with the ability to affirmatively seek therapies, but at what cost? I also believe that autistic children would be more likely than they are now to suffer disproportionately from abuse. Additionally this type of screening would further stigmatize children who have autism.
tfa_explain_freescreen: If I didn't have the lived experience that I have now I would likely proceed with the test, but at what cost? As a person with lived experience I would not want my younger self to be allowed to make that choice without the knowledge, beliefs, and lived experience that I have now.
tfa_whylater: I believe that linguistic and behavioral observations would allow a properly trained medical doctor to make a diagnosis that would be much more reliable and valid than any test might provide at the age of 4 months.
tfa_feedback_other: It's a complex issue and I'm glad that  the potential outcomes are being considered. I would like to receive and have the opportunity to inspect the resulting quantitative data along with qualitative responses

tfa_ethic_details: I believe it is somewhat ethical for a doctor to offer to parents, though this may cause the parents to be scared for no reason if everything is progressing. It is morally wrong to give it with no concerns as the skills at this age vary greatly.

So if the test showed they had autism and we started therapy early on and then find out they didn't have it than to me the only thing that was different from maybe how my other kids might be raised is that maybe early intervention was started. fif_diag_firstsigns_what: Delayed speech and sensory processing issues
tfa_comments_test: It would depend on the method of testing as to how I would feel exactly. As long as there was no harm to my baby. I would feel relieved because an earlier diagnosis could help me understand my child/baby better.
tfa_ethic_details: Again, it depends on the types of screening and the effects on the babies. I do see an issue with offering screening. I believe it should be at the discretion of the parents.
tfa_comments_regrets: I have an autistic 9 year old. We have had him in therapy for the last 5 years. Although he is not neurotypical, he goes to school with and can interact well with neurotypical children. It is worth the time and sacrifice to get help early.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: The test may have given false hope.
tfa_explain_freescreen: I have a child that is autistic and would want to know as early as possible.
tfa_whylater: Bay is old and had more time to watch for developmental issues.

fif_work_explain: I work for an orthodontist and we see patients with autism regularly.

tfa_ethic_details: A metric for deciding "if we should screen" feels like it would be too subjective. Then you move into "why did my baby get screened but not my neighbors?" For me, this is not equitable. Every baby gets screened and that way every baby has equal access to early interventions and parents to eduction.
tfa_comments_regrets: It's possible the early interventions and therapies helped shape and mold my kid.
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: I may have trusted the results too much and ignored signs (or brushed them off) at a younger age than 5 therefore not getting them early intervention.
tfa_explain_freescreen: Knowledge is power
occup: I work in a church with youth in grades 6-12

fif_needs_list: gross motor delays, nonverbal autism, moderate to severe disabilities, MECP2 Duplication syndrome
fif_signs_age: when he/she was younger than 6 months old
fif_diag_firstsigns_what: no eye contact, low muscle tone
tfa_comments_test: happy that this exists, curious about how effective it is, and wonder about the training of the test administrators and test developers (e.g. if baby has a bad day/ is fussy, does it affect results, etc). Could a "no autism detected" outcome of the test result in a lack of access to resources or more advocating down the road to receive needed ASD resources?
tfa_ethic_details: as long as there is a clear explanation that there are no behaviors or observations prompting the offer it is OK.
tfa_comments_gain_loss: answers are everything, the not-knowing is what is so hard mentally and emotionally. Also, formal diagnoses are required for any therapy services to be offered, so getting answers and the diagnosis helped us get access to services we would not have had otherwise.
tfa_comments_regrets: I would be very disappointed to learn this for a number of reasons. In our case, for example, an early misdiagnosis of hearing loss meant losing almost a year of finding real answers for our son's delays, not to mention the grief process and acceptance and lifestyle changes we made based on the original false diagnosis. Providers should be very certain before giving diagnoses to prevent further trauma to families.
tfa_comments_gained_lost: reassurance, peace
tfa_comments_regrets_pos: early intervention is everything
tfa_explain_freescreen: we always value answers. 4 months is super early though. I wonder what the test would entail.

tfa_ethic_details: I think the decision should be made by the parents and should not be targeted since there are little to no markers at that age. There is nothing wrong with offering something and communicating why it is being offered.
tfa_explain_freescreen: I think the knowledge would far outweigh the chance that the result is incorrect. And measures could be taken while the child is young. Plus it can help us better understand autism at a young age.

fif_diag_firstsigns_what: Wouldnt answer to his name

tfa_ethic_details: Everyone wants to know
tfa_comments_gain_loss: Gain the knowledge, but you just have to put up with the stigma from older parents and family
tfa_comments_regrets: Because I did what I could for my child no matter what the results of the test and if they turn out Neurotypical, that's great
tfa_comments_gained_lost: Nothing changed
